feedback.text: I would like to express my full support for the Stop Killing Games initiative, which seeks to ensure that consumers can retain access to online multiplayer games, even if the company decides to close down its servers. This is essential to protect players’ rights and ensure that digital purchases maintain their real value. In the current situation, companies can unilaterally decide to shut down servers or online services, leaving players without access to products they have already paid for. This is a clear injustice for consumers, as it is a good for which a legitimate economic investment has been made. It should not be possible, after having purchased a product, to disappear without prior notice and with no alternative to continue using it. Many games have disappeared or closed in an abrupt and definitive manner, leaving players without access to products for which they had already paid. For example, Anthem de Electronic Arts was closed less than three years after its launch, eliminating all progress, virtual objects and additional content acquired by players. These sudden closures not only completely obscure the game experience, but also destroy communities and social ties that had formed around it, generating great frustration among consumers. The lack of preservation alternatives demonstrates how unfair it is that a company can unilaterally decide to remove a digital product, leaving users without the way to enjoy what they legitimately purchased. The loss of access has several negative effects on players. First, the experience for which it was paid is completely eliminated, including content, progress, achievements and elements acquired within the game. All time, effort and money invested is lost, with no possibility of compensation. This creates an unfair consumption scenario, where users do not have control over the durability of their purchase. Moreover, closing down services abruptly affects the social dimension of video games. Many players create communities, friendships and interaction networks that depend on the continuity of online gambling. As servers disappear, not only access to content is lost, but also the social links that have been created, which is an additional harm to the consumer. Another consequence is the lack of trust in the digital industry. Consumers could hesitate to make future digital purchases if they know that any product could disappear unilaterally, cancelling the investment made. This directly affects the perception of security and justice in the digital market and limits the development of lasting relationships between consumers and businesses. The Stop Killing Games initiative aims to correct these injustices, ensuring that consumers have the right to keep the products they have paid for. This not only protects players, but also sets a standard of fairness and transparency in the digital market. Consumers should be able to enjoy their purchases without fear of disappearing due to economic decisions of third parties. In conclusion, supporting this initiative means defending fairness in digital consumption, protecting the economic investment and time of players, preserving the social dimension of online games and strengthening trust in industry. I urge the European Commission to support the Stop Killing Games initiative, ensuring that consumer rights are effectively protected in the digital environment.
